question: A reaction is said to be reversible when it

options.A: proceeds only in the forward direction
options.B: can proceed in both forward and backward directions under suitable conditions

answer_text: can proceed in both forward and backward directions under suitable conditions

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct statement about reversible reactions.
explanation.principle: A reversible reaction can move from reactants to products and from products back to reactants when suitable conditions are maintained.
steps: In an ordinary forward reaction, reactants change to products.
In a reversible reaction, the products are still able to react and form the original reactants again.
Because both directions are possible, the reaction may later reach equilibrium in a closed system.
So the correct choice is the one stating that can proceed in both forward and backward directions under suitable conditions.
explanation.final_answer: B. can proceed in both forward and backward directions under suitable conditions
explanation.simple_explanation: A reversible reaction is one that can go forward and backward. That is the central idea behind this question. To strengthen this idea further, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson and more worked examples.

question: For the equilibrium N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) ⇌ 2NH₃(g), increasing the pressure will favour

options.A: the side with more gas molecules
options.B: the side with fewer gas molecules
options.C: neither side because pressure has no effect

answer_text: the side with fewer gas molecules

explanation.question_restate: We are to determine how a change in condition affects the position of equilibrium.
explanation.principle: The equilibrium position depends mainly on concentration, temperature, and pressure for gaseous systems. A catalyst does not change the position; it only helps equilibrium to be reached faster.
steps: At equilibrium, the system can respond to disturbances.
In this question, the important condition is: Pressure change affects gaseous equilibria by favouring the side with fewer moles of gas.
The system shifts in the direction that tends to reduce the effect of the change imposed on it.
Therefore, the correct option is the one stating that the equilibrium shifts the side with fewer gas molecules.
explanation.final_answer: B. the side with fewer gas molecules
explanation.simple_explanation: To answer questions of this type, always ask yourself: what change was introduced, and which direction reduces that change most effectively? To strengthen this idea further, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson and more worked examples.

question: For an exothermic equilibrium reaction, increasing temperature usually shifts the equilibrium

explanation.question_restate: We are to determine how a change in condition affects the position of equilibrium.
explanation.principle: The equilibrium position depends mainly on concentration, temperature, and pressure for gaseous systems. A catalyst does not change the position; it only helps equilibrium to be reached faster.
steps: At equilibrium, the system can respond to disturbances.
In this question, the important condition is: Heat can be treated like a product in an exothermic reaction.
The system shifts in the direction that tends to reduce the effect of the change imposed on it.
Therefore, the correct option is the one stating that the equilibrium shifts towards the endothermic direction.
explanation.final_answer: B. towards the endothermic direction
explanation.simple_explanation: To answer questions of this type, always ask yourself: what change was introduced, and which direction reduces that change most effectively? To strengthen this idea further, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson and more worked examples.

question: Dynamic equilibrium is best described as a state in which

options.A: all visible change has stopped and no reaction occurs
options.B: reactants and products are present, and both forward and backward reactions continue at equal rates
options.D: the reactants are equal in mass to the products

answer_text: reactants and products are present, and both forward and backward reactions continue at equal rates

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct meaning or property of dynamic equilibrium.
explanation.principle: Dynamic equilibrium is not a dead stop. It is a balanced state in which opposing reactions continue at the same rate, so measurable properties stay constant.
steps: The forward reaction keeps converting reactants to products.
At the same time, the backward reaction keeps converting products back to reactants.
When the two rates become equal, there is no net overall change in observable quantities.
That is why the correct statement is that reactants and products are present, and both forward and backward reactions continue at equal rates.
explanation.final_answer: B. reactants and products are present, and both forward and backward reactions continue at equal rates
explanation.simple_explanation: A good memory aid is this: equilibrium looks still to the eye, but it is active at the particle level. To strengthen this idea further, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson and more worked examples.

question: Le Chatelier’s principle states that when a system at equilibrium is disturbed, the system

options.B: adjusts in a direction that opposes the disturbance

answer_text: adjusts in a direction that opposes the disturbance

explanation.question_restate: We are to apply Le Chatelier’s principle to predict the response of an equilibrium system.
explanation.principle: Le Chatelier’s principle says that if an equilibrium system is disturbed by a change in concentration, pressure, or temperature, the system shifts to reduce that disturbance.
steps: First identify the disturbance introduced into the system.
Then ask which direction of shift would reduce the effect of that disturbance.
Remember that heat may be treated like a reactant or product depending on whether the forward reaction is endothermic or exothermic.
Using that reasoning, the correct answer is that the system shifts adjusts in a direction that opposes the disturbance.
explanation.final_answer: B. adjusts in a direction that opposes the disturbance
explanation.simple_explanation: This principle is one of the most tested ideas in equilibrium. Focus on the direction of shift, not on memorising isolated answers. To strengthen this idea further, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson and more worked examples.

explanation.principle: Le Chatelier’s principle says that if an equilibrium system is disturbed by a change in concentration, pressure, or temperature, the system shifts to reduce that disturbance.
steps: First identify the disturbance introduced into the system.
Then ask which direction of shift would reduce the effect of that disturbance.
Remember that heat may be treated like a reactant or product depending on whether the forward reaction is endothermic or exothermic.
Using that reasoning, the correct answer is that the system shifts the forward reaction.
explanation.final_answer: A. the forward reaction
explanation.simple_explanation: This principle is one of the most tested ideas in equilibrium. Focus on the direction of shift, not on memorising isolated answers. To strengthen this idea further, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson and more worked examples.

options.B: adjusts in a direction that opposes the disturbance

answer_text: adjusts in a direction that opposes the disturbance

question: Using Le Chatelier’s idea, if more product is added to a system at equilibrium, the position of equilibrium usually shifts

options.D: nowhere, because product has no effect

explanation.question_restate: We are to apply Le Chatelier’s principle to predict the response of an equilibrium system.

answer_text: the side with more gas molecules

question: For the reaction aA + bB ⇌ cC + dD, the equilibrium constant expression is

explanation.question_restate: We are to interpret the meaning or expression of the equilibrium constant, Kc.
explanation.principle: The equilibrium constant compares the concentrations of products and reactants at equilibrium, each raised to the power of its stoichiometric coefficient.
steps: Write the balanced reversible equation first.
Place product concentrations in the numerator and reactant concentrations in the denominator.
Raise each concentration term to the coefficient shown in the balanced equation.
Therefore the correct response is [C]^c[D]^d/[A]^a[B]^b.
explanation.simple_explanation: For JAMB-level questions, the major points are the correct form of Kc, what a large or small Kc means, and the fact that temperature affects its value. To strengthen this idea further, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson and more worked examples.
explanation.diagram:
For aA + bB ⇌ cC + dD
Kc = [C]^c[D]^d / [A]^a[B]^b

subtopic: Simple examples: action of steam on iron
question: When red-hot iron reacts with steam, the products are

explanation.question_restate: We are to apply equilibrium ideas to the action of steam on iron.
explanation.principle: When steam passes over red-hot iron, iron is oxidized and hydrogen is produced. This common syllabus example helps students connect equilibrium with real chemical change.
steps: Steam here acts as the oxidizing agent in the forward direction.
Hot iron reacts to form an iron oxide, commonly written as Fe₃O₄ in the balanced school-level equation.
Hydrogen gas is produced; if conditions favour the reverse direction, hydrogen can help reduce the oxide.
So the correct answer is iron oxide and hydrogen.
explanation.final_answer: B. iron oxide and hydrogen
explanation.simple_explanation: This example is important because JAMB likes using familiar reactions to test equilibrium concepts, not just bare definitions. To strengthen this idea further, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson and more worked examples.

subtopic: Simple examples: action of steam on iron
question: The reaction between steam and hot iron is represented by

explanation.question_restate: We are to apply equilibrium ideas to the action of steam on iron.
explanation.principle: When steam passes over red-hot iron, iron is oxidized and hydrogen is produced. This common syllabus example helps students connect equilibrium with real chemical change.
steps: Steam here acts as the oxidizing agent in the forward direction.
Hot iron reacts to form an iron oxide, commonly written as Fe₃O₄ in the balanced school-level equation.
Hydrogen gas is produced; if conditions favour the reverse direction, hydrogen can help reduce the oxide.
So the correct answer is 3Fe + 4H₂O(g) ⇌ Fe₃O₄ + 4H₂.
explanation.final_answer: B. 3Fe + 4H₂O(g) ⇌ Fe₃O₄ + 4H₂
explanation.simple_explanation: This example is important because JAMB likes using familiar reactions to test equilibrium concepts, not just bare definitions. To strengthen this idea further, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson and more worked examples.
explanation.diagram:
3Fe + 4H₂O(g) ⇌ Fe₃O₄ + 4H₂
forward: steam oxidizes hot iron
backward: hydrogen can reduce the oxide under suitable conditions

steps: Steam here acts as the oxidizing agent in the forward direction.
Hot iron reacts to form an iron oxide, commonly written as Fe₃O₄ in the balanced school-level equation.
Hydrogen gas is produced; if conditions favour the reverse direction, hydrogen can help reduce the oxide.
So the correct answer is a reversible redox equilibrium.
explanation.final_answer: B. a reversible redox equilibrium
explanation.simple_explanation: This example is important because JAMB likes using familiar reactions to test equilibrium concepts, not just bare definitions. To strengthen this idea further, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson and more worked examples.

question: At equilibrium in a closed system, which pair of statements is correct?

options.A: Concentrations are always equal; reactions stop
options.B: Forward and backward rates are equal; measurable properties remain constant
options.C: Only products are present; backward reaction stops
options.D: Catalyst shifts equilibrium; temperature does not

answer_text: Forward and backward rates are equal; measurable properties remain constant

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose the statement that correctly combines key ideas from chemical equilibrium.
explanation.principle: JAMB often mixes definitions, examples, and consequences of equilibrium in one item. The safe approach is to test each option against the core principles of reversibility, equal rates at equilibrium, and response to changes.
steps: Check whether the option agrees with the idea of a closed system for equilibrium.
Check whether it respects the fact that forward and backward reactions continue in dynamic equilibrium.
Check whether it treats Kc correctly as temperature-dependent and catalyst-independent.
Therefore, the correct option is Forward and backward rates are equal; measurable properties remain constant.
explanation.final_answer: B. Forward and backward rates are equal; measurable properties remain constant
explanation.simple_explanation: Mixed questions test understanding more than memory. Once the principles are clear, such items become easier. To strengthen this idea further, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson and more worked examples.

options.A: A reversible reaction may attain a state in which both directions continue at equal rates
options.B: A reversible reaction must stop before equilibrium is reached
options.C: Dynamic equilibrium occurs only in open vessels
options.D: At equilibrium all molecules become identical

answer_text: A reversible reaction may attain a state in which both directions continue at equal rates

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose the statement that correctly combines key ideas from chemical equilibrium.
explanation.principle: JAMB often mixes definitions, examples, and consequences of equilibrium in one item. The safe approach is to test each option against the core principles of reversibility, equal rates at equilibrium, and response to changes.
steps: Check whether the option agrees with the idea of a closed system for equilibrium.

question: Which condition is most necessary if a reversible reaction is to become established in the laboratory?

options.A: The products must be allowed to escape continuously
options.B: The reaction should occur in an open dish
options.C: The reacting system should be kept closed so that products can remain in contact with reactants
options.D: The temperature must be reduced to 0°C

answer_text: The reacting system should be kept closed so that products can remain in contact with reactants

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the condition that allows the backward reaction to occur as well as the forward reaction.
explanation.principle: For reversibility to be observed, products must remain in the system so they can react back to form reactants.
steps: In a reversible reaction, products are not simply removed forever.
If products escape, only the forward reaction is favoured in practice.
A closed system keeps both reactants and products available.
That is why a closed system is important for establishing reversibility.
explanation.final_answer: C. The reacting system should be kept closed so that products can remain in contact with reactants
explanation.simple_explanation: A reversible reaction can go in both directions under suitable conditions. Keep that central idea in mind. To understand it more deeply, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: Which statement best distinguishes a reversible reaction from an irreversible one?

options.A: A reversible reaction may proceed in both directions under the same set of conditions
options.B: A reversible reaction always goes to completion
options.C: A reversible reaction must involve gases only
options.D: A reversible reaction can never produce a visible change

answer_text: A reversible reaction may proceed in both directions under the same set of conditions

explanation.question_restate: We are to state the main feature that separates reversible reactions from irreversible reactions.
explanation.principle: The hallmark of reversibility is the ability of products to change back into reactants under suitable conditions.
steps: Irreversible reactions mainly proceed in one direction.
Reversible reactions can proceed forward and backward.
This can happen under the same overall conditions once both reactants and products are present.
So the correct distinguishing feature is two-way change.
explanation.final_answer: A. A reversible reaction may proceed in both directions under the same set of conditions
explanation.simple_explanation: A reversible reaction can go in both directions under suitable conditions. Keep that central idea in mind. To understand it more deeply, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: The symbol ⇌ in a chemical equation indicates that the reaction

explanation.question_restate: We are to interpret the meaning of the symbol ⇌ in a chemical equation.
explanation.principle: Chemists use special symbols to convey reaction behaviour; ⇌ shows forward and backward change.
steps: A single arrow usually shows one main direction.
The double opposing arrows indicate both directions are possible.
That is the accepted symbol for a reversible reaction.
Therefore the equation represents a reversible change.
explanation.final_answer: B. is reversible
explanation.simple_explanation: A reversible reaction can go in both directions under suitable conditions. Keep that central idea in mind. To understand it more deeply, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: A reaction carried out in a sealed tube is more likely to show reversibility than one carried out in an open vessel because the sealed tube

options.B: keeps products from escaping and allows the backward reaction to occur
options.D: changes the stoichiometric ratio of reactants

answer_text: keeps products from escaping and allows the backward reaction to occur

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why a sealed tube often helps a reversible reaction to be observed.
explanation.principle: The backward reaction needs product molecules to remain available in the reaction mixture.
steps: In an open vessel, volatile products may escape.
If they escape, they cannot react back.
A sealed tube holds the substances together and makes reverse change possible.
So the sealed tube encourages reversible behaviour to be established.
explanation.final_answer: B. keeps products from escaping and allows the backward reaction to occur
explanation.simple_explanation: A reversible reaction can go in both directions under suitable conditions. Keep that central idea in mind. To understand it more deeply, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: Which of the following processes is best described as reversible?

options.B: Neutralization that goes completely to salt and water
options.C: Formation and decomposition of NH₄Cl in a closed vessel
options.D: Rusting of iron exposed to moist air

answer_text: Formation and decomposition of NH₄Cl in a closed vessel

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a process that can go forward and backward under suitable conditions.
explanation.principle: Some systems can form products and also reform the original substances when conditions allow.
steps: Burning and rusting are usually treated as irreversible in school chemistry.
Formation and decomposition of ammonium chloride can occur in opposite directions in a closed setup.
That makes it a good example of reversibility.
Hence option C is correct.
explanation.final_answer: C. Formation and decomposition of NH₄Cl in a closed vessel
explanation.simple_explanation: A reversible reaction can go in both directions under suitable conditions. Keep that central idea in mind. To understand it more deeply, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: Why is the reaction H₂(g) + I₂(g) ⇌ 2HI(g) classified as reversible?

options.A: Because hydrogen iodide formed can decompose again to give H₂ and I₂
options.C: Because it must occur only in sunlight
options.D: Because iodine is a coloured substance

answer_text: Because hydrogen iodide formed can decompose again to give H₂ and I₂

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why the hydrogen–iodine reaction is called reversible.
explanation.principle: A reaction is reversible when the products can react to regenerate the original reactants.
steps: Hydrogen and iodine combine to form hydrogen iodide.
Hydrogen iodide can also decompose back to hydrogen and iodine under suitable conditions.
This shows both forward and backward directions are possible.
Therefore the reaction is reversible.
explanation.final_answer: A. Because hydrogen iodide formed can decompose again to give H₂ and I₂
explanation.simple_explanation: A reversible reaction can go in both directions under suitable conditions. Keep that central idea in mind. To understand it more deeply, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: In a reversible reaction at equilibrium, the reactants

options.B: are still present together with the products
options.C: must be in a different container from the products

answer_text: are still present together with the products

explanation.question_restate: We are to state what happens to reactants in a reversible system when equilibrium is reached.
explanation.principle: Equilibrium in a reversible system does not mean complete disappearance of one side.
steps: The forward reaction does not consume every reactant totally.
The backward reaction reforms some reactants from products.
So both reactants and products are present together.
That is why equilibrium mixtures contain both sides.
explanation.final_answer: B. are still present together with the products
explanation.simple_explanation: A reversible reaction can go in both directions under suitable conditions. Keep that central idea in mind. To understand it more deeply, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: A student says, 'Once a reaction is reversible, the amounts of reactants and products must always become equal.' The best correction is that

options.A: the statement is true for every reversible reaction
options.B: reversibility means both directions are possible, not necessarily that amounts become equal
options.C: equal amounts are required before a reaction can start
options.D: only gases can form reversible systems

answer_text: reversibility means both directions are possible, not necessarily that amounts become equal

explanation.question_restate: We are to correct a common misunderstanding about reversible reactions.
explanation.principle: Reversibility is about direction of change, not compulsory equality of amounts.
steps: A reversible reaction can go forward and backward.
The final composition depends on conditions and the equilibrium position.
There is no rule that reactants and products must be equal in amount.
Therefore option B is the correct correction.
explanation.final_answer: B. reversibility means both directions are possible, not necessarily that amounts become equal
explanation.simple_explanation: A reversible reaction can go in both directions under suitable conditions. Keep that central idea in mind. To understand it more deeply, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: Which observation would most strongly suggest that a reaction may be reversible?

options.A: A product once formed remains permanently removed from the system
options.B: Under suitable conditions, the product can regenerate the original reactant
options.C: The reaction occurs only with a spark

answer_text: Under suitable conditions, the product can regenerate the original reactant

explanation.question_restate: We are to pick the observation that points to reversibility.
explanation.principle: If products can change back into reactants, the reaction is reversible.
steps: Reversibility requires the possibility of backward change.
Heat evolution alone does not prove reversibility.
Regeneration of reactants from products is direct evidence of reverse reaction.
Hence option B is the best sign.
explanation.final_answer: B. Under suitable conditions, the product can regenerate the original reactant
explanation.simple_explanation: A reversible reaction can go in both directions under suitable conditions. Keep that central idea in mind. To understand it more deeply, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: Which statement about a reversible reaction in a closed flask is correct?

options.A: Only the forward reaction can occur because the flask is closed
options.B: Only the backward reaction can occur because products are trapped
options.C: Both forward and backward reactions may occur until equilibrium is established
options.D: No reaction occurs once the flask is closed

answer_text: Both forward and backward reactions may occur until equilibrium is established

explanation.question_restate: We are to determine what happens in a closed flask containing a reversible system.
explanation.principle: A closed system allows products to remain, so both directions can take place.
steps: Initially the forward reaction may dominate if only reactants are present.
As products appear, the backward reaction becomes possible.
Both directions continue until equilibrium is reached.
Therefore option C describes the system correctly.
explanation.final_answer: C. Both forward and backward reactions may occur until equilibrium is established
explanation.simple_explanation: A reversible reaction can go in both directions under suitable conditions. Keep that central idea in mind. To understand it more deeply, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: Which pair of arrows correctly represents a reversible process?

explanation.question_restate: We are simply to choose the notation for reversibility.
explanation.principle: Double opposing arrows are the accepted notation for a reversible reaction.
steps: One arrow shows one principal direction.
A reversible process needs two opposite directions to be shown.
The notation that does this is ⇌.
So option C is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: A reversible reaction can go in both directions under suitable conditions. Keep that central idea in mind. To understand it more deeply, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: The decomposition of a product back to its reactants in the same vessel is evidence of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the concept shown when products change back to reactants in the same vessel.
explanation.principle: Reverse formation of reactants from products is the essence of reversibility.
steps: If the product decomposes to the original substances, backward reaction is occurring.
That means the system can operate in two directions.
This is not merely catalysis or electrolysis.
Hence it is evidence of reversibility.
explanation.final_answer: C. reversibility
explanation.simple_explanation: A reversible reaction can go in both directions under suitable conditions. Keep that central idea in mind. To understand it more deeply, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: In school chemistry, reversible reactions are commonly studied under conditions where

options.A: the products are continuously bubbled away
options.B: reactants and products remain together long enough to interact
options.C: the vessel is left uncovered to the atmosphere
options.D: one reactant is removed every minute

answer_text: reactants and products remain together long enough to interact

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the general condition under which reversible reactions are best studied.
explanation.principle: Forward and backward reactions require continued contact among the substances involved.
steps: If products are removed, reverse change becomes difficult.
If everything remains together, both directions can happen.
This is why chemists often use closed or controlled systems.
So option B is correct.
explanation.final_answer: B. reactants and products remain together long enough to interact
explanation.simple_explanation: A reversible reaction can go in both directions under suitable conditions. Keep that central idea in mind. To understand it more deeply, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: Which statement is NOT a valid feature of a reversible reaction?

options.A: It may reach a state of equilibrium
options.B: The products may react to reform reactants
options.C: It necessarily goes to completion in one direction only
options.D: It is usually represented with opposing arrows

answer_text: It necessarily goes to completion in one direction only

explanation.question_restate: We are to find the statement that does not fit a reversible reaction.
explanation.principle: A reversible reaction does not simply go one way to completion under all conditions.
steps: Reversible reactions can establish equilibrium.
They can regenerate reactants from products.
They are represented by opposing arrows.
The statement about one-way completion is therefore the invalid one.
explanation.final_answer: C. It necessarily goes to completion in one direction only
explanation.simple_explanation: A reversible reaction can go in both directions under suitable conditions. Keep that central idea in mind. To understand it more deeply, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: When NH₄Cl(s) is heated in a closed tube, NH₃(g) and HCl(g) are formed, and on cooling NH₄Cl(s) reforms. This demonstrates

explanation.question_restate: We are to interpret the heating and cooling behaviour of ammonium chloride in a closed tube.
explanation.principle: A change that can be reversed to give back the starting substance is a reversible change.
steps: Heating ammonium chloride can produce ammonia and hydrogen chloride gases.
When conditions change, these can combine again to form ammonium chloride.
That shows the system can move in both directions.
Therefore it demonstrates reversibility.
explanation.final_answer: C. a reversible chemical change
explanation.simple_explanation: A reversible reaction can go in both directions under suitable conditions. Keep that central idea in mind. To understand it more deeply, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: Why are many textbook reversible reactions discussed together with the idea of equilibrium?

options.A: Because every irreversible reaction reaches equilibrium too
options.B: Because reversible reactions can settle into a balanced state where both directions still occur
options.C: Because equilibrium means the reaction has been stopped artificially
options.D: Because equilibrium and reversibility are unrelated ideas

answer_text: Because reversible reactions can settle into a balanced state where both directions still occur

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the close connection between reversibility and equilibrium.
explanation.principle: Equilibrium is possible because in a reversible reaction both forward and backward reactions occur.
steps: Without a backward reaction, there can be no true chemical equilibrium.
Reversible reactions allow both directions to continue.
At equilibrium, the two directions become balanced in rate.
That is why the two ideas are usually discussed together.
explanation.final_answer: B. Because reversible reactions can settle into a balanced state where both directions still occur
explanation.simple_explanation: A reversible reaction can go in both directions under suitable conditions. Keep that central idea in mind. To understand it more deeply, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: For the equilibrium N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) ⇌ 2NH₃(g), an increase in pressure at constant temperature will favour

options.A: the left-hand side because it has fewer compounds
options.B: the right-hand side because it has fewer moles of gas
options.C: neither side because pressure affects rate only
options.D: the side with the larger volume of gas

answer_text: the right-hand side because it has fewer moles of gas

explanation.question_restate: We are to predict the effect of increased pressure on a gaseous equilibrium.
explanation.principle: A rise in pressure favours the side with fewer gaseous molecules because that side occupies less volume.
steps: Count the gaseous moles on each side.
Left side has 4 moles of gas; right side has 2 moles of gas.
Higher pressure favours the side with fewer gas molecules.
So the equilibrium shifts to the right.
explanation.final_answer: B. the right-hand side because it has fewer moles of gas
explanation.simple_explanation: When equilibrium is disturbed, the position shifts in the direction that tends to oppose the change. That is the key exam idea here. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: If the concentration of H₂ in the equilibrium N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) ⇌ 2NH₃(g) is increased, the equilibrium position will move

options.A: to the right to use up some of the added H₂
options.B: to the left to produce more H₂
options.C: nowhere because concentration has no effect

answer_text: to the right to use up some of the added H₂

explanation.question_restate: We are to predict what happens when more hydrogen is added to the equilibrium mixture.
explanation.principle: Increasing the concentration of a reactant shifts equilibrium toward the side that consumes that reactant.
steps: The added substance is H₂, which is a reactant.
The system responds by using up part of the extra hydrogen.
That means more ammonia is formed.
So the equilibrium shifts to the right.
explanation.final_answer: A. to the right to use up some of the added H₂
explanation.simple_explanation: When equilibrium is disturbed, the position shifts in the direction that tends to oppose the change. That is the key exam idea here. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: In the equilibrium 2SO₂(g) + O₂(g) ⇌ 2SO₃(g), decreasing the volume of the vessel will favour

options.A: the forward reaction because the product side has fewer moles of gas
options.B: the backward reaction because volume reduction always causes decomposition
options.C: neither direction because volume is not a factor
options.D: the side with more moles of gas

answer_text: the forward reaction because the product side has fewer moles of gas

explanation.question_restate: We are to determine the effect of decreasing vessel volume on a gaseous equilibrium.
explanation.principle: Reducing volume increases pressure, so the side with fewer gas moles is favoured.
steps: Left side contains 3 moles of gas in total.
Right side contains 2 moles of gas.
A smaller volume favours the side with fewer gas molecules.
Hence the equilibrium shifts to the right.
explanation.final_answer: A. the forward reaction because the product side has fewer moles of gas
explanation.simple_explanation: When equilibrium is disturbed, the position shifts in the direction that tends to oppose the change. That is the key exam idea here. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: For an exothermic forward reaction, increasing temperature generally shifts the equilibrium

options.D: only if a catalyst is added

explanation.question_restate: We are to state how temperature affects the equilibrium of an exothermic forward reaction.
explanation.principle: Heat can be treated like a product in an exothermic forward reaction, so adding heat favours the reverse direction.
steps: Exothermic means the forward reaction releases heat.
Raising temperature is like adding a product.
The system shifts to reduce that addition by favouring the endothermic direction.
Therefore it shifts towards the reactants.
explanation.final_answer: B. towards the reactants
explanation.simple_explanation: When equilibrium is disturbed, the position shifts in the direction that tends to oppose the change. That is the key exam idea here. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: For an endothermic forward reaction, lowering the temperature will favour

explanation.question_restate: We are to predict the effect of lowering temperature on an endothermic forward reaction.
explanation.principle: Cooling favours the exothermic direction, which is the reverse of an endothermic forward reaction.
steps: The forward reaction absorbs heat because it is endothermic.
When temperature is lowered, the system tends to produce heat.
That means it shifts towards the exothermic reverse direction.
So the equilibrium moves backward.
explanation.final_answer: B. the reverse direction
explanation.simple_explanation: When equilibrium is disturbed, the position shifts in the direction that tends to oppose the change. That is the key exam idea here. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Which factor can change the equilibrium position of a gaseous system only when the number of gaseous moles differs on the two sides?

options.D: Surface area of a solid catalyst

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the factor whose effect depends on unequal gas mole numbers.
explanation.principle: Pressure changes equilibrium position only when the two sides contain different total gaseous moles.
steps: If both sides have equal gaseous moles, pressure change affects them similarly.
When the mole numbers differ, one side is favoured under pressure change.
That factor is pressure.
So option A is correct.
explanation.final_answer: A. Pressure
explanation.simple_explanation: When equilibrium is disturbed, the position shifts in the direction that tends to oppose the change. That is the key exam idea here. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: In the equilibrium H₂(g) + I₂(g) ⇌ 2HI(g), changing the pressure at constant temperature has little effect on the equilibrium position because

options.B: the number of moles of gas is the same on both sides

answer_text: the number of moles of gas is the same on both sides

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why pressure has little or no effect on this gaseous equilibrium.
explanation.principle: Pressure influences equilibrium position mainly when total gaseous mole numbers differ between the two sides.
steps: Left side has 2 moles of gas in total: 1 mole H₂ and 1 mole I₂.
Right side also has 2 moles of HI gas.
Since gaseous mole numbers are equal, pressure change gives no directional advantage.
Thus the equilibrium position is little changed.
explanation.final_answer: B. the number of moles of gas is the same on both sides
explanation.simple_explanation: When equilibrium is disturbed, the position shifts in the direction that tends to oppose the change. That is the key exam idea here. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: When extra product is added to a system at equilibrium, the equilibrium position usually shifts

options.A: towards the side that forms even more product
options.B: towards the side that uses up some of the added product

answer_text: towards the side that uses up some of the added product

explanation.question_restate: We are to state the general effect of adding more product to an equilibrium mixture.
explanation.principle: The system responds to added product by shifting in the direction that consumes some of it.
steps: Addition of product disturbs the equilibrium.
A shift occurs to reduce the disturbance.
The reverse direction uses up product and forms reactants.
Therefore the equilibrium shifts backward.
explanation.final_answer: B. towards the side that uses up some of the added product
explanation.simple_explanation: When equilibrium is disturbed, the position shifts in the direction that tends to oppose the change. That is the key exam idea here. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Which change would increase the equilibrium yield of SO₃ in 2SO₂(g) + O₂(g) ⇌ 2SO₃(g), where the forward reaction is exothermic?

options.D: High temperature and high pressure only if no catalyst is present

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose the conditions that favour product formation in an exothermic gaseous equilibrium.
explanation.principle: For an exothermic reaction forming fewer gas moles, lower temperature and higher pressure favour products.
steps: The forward reaction is exothermic, so lower temperature favours it.
The product side has fewer gaseous moles than the reactant side.
High pressure favours the side with fewer gas molecules.
So low temperature and high pressure give greater yield of SO₃.
explanation.final_answer: B. Low temperature and high pressure
explanation.simple_explanation: When equilibrium is disturbed, the position shifts in the direction that tends to oppose the change. That is the key exam idea here. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Which one of the following changes cannot shift the position of equilibrium?

options.D: Changing pressure in a suitable gaseous system

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the change that does not alter the equilibrium position.
explanation.principle: A catalyst changes the speed of reaching equilibrium but not the final position of equilibrium.
steps: Concentration, temperature, and sometimes pressure can disturb equilibrium position.
A catalyst lowers activation energy for both forward and backward reactions.
So equilibrium is reached faster but in the same position.
Therefore adding a catalyst does not shift the equilibrium.
explanation.final_answer: C. Adding a catalyst
explanation.simple_explanation: When equilibrium is disturbed, the position shifts in the direction that tends to oppose the change. That is the key exam idea here. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: For the equilibrium CaCO₃(s) ⇌ CaO(s) + CO₂(g), increasing the pressure of the system tends to favour

options.C: neither side because solids are present
options.D: the side with the higher mass

explanation.question_restate: We are to predict the effect of pressure increase on an equilibrium involving one gaseous product.
explanation.principle: Higher pressure favours the side with fewer moles of gas.
steps: Left side has no gaseous moles shown in the equilibrium expression.
Right side has 1 mole of CO₂ gas.
Increasing pressure opposes gas formation and favours the side with fewer gaseous particles.
So the equilibrium shifts toward CaCO₃ formation.
explanation.final_answer: B. formation of CaCO₃
explanation.simple_explanation: When equilibrium is disturbed, the position shifts in the direction that tends to oppose the change. That is the key exam idea here. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: In a gaseous equilibrium, removal of one of the products will usually

options.A: shift the equilibrium towards product formation
options.D: make the equilibrium constant become zero

answer_text: shift the equilibrium towards product formation

explanation.question_restate: We are to decide what happens when product is removed from a system at equilibrium.
explanation.principle: Removing product disturbs the equilibrium, so the system shifts to replace some of the removed product.
steps: Product concentration falls below its equilibrium value.
The forward reaction is then favoured to rebuild product.
This means the equilibrium moves towards product formation.
Hence option A is correct.
explanation.final_answer: A. shift the equilibrium towards product formation
explanation.simple_explanation: When equilibrium is disturbed, the position shifts in the direction that tends to oppose the change. That is the key exam idea here. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: For a reaction in which the forward process is endothermic and produces more moles of gas, which condition favours the products?

options.D: High temperature and addition of catalyst only

explanation.question_restate: We are to combine the effects of temperature and pressure on an equilibrium system.
explanation.principle: An endothermic forward reaction is favoured by high temperature, while the side with more gas moles is favoured by low pressure.
steps: High temperature favours the endothermic forward direction.
Low pressure favours the side with more gaseous molecules.
Since the products are said to have more gas moles, both changes favour products.
So option B is correct.
explanation.final_answer: B. High temperature and low pressure
explanation.simple_explanation: When equilibrium is disturbed, the position shifts in the direction that tends to oppose the change. That is the key exam idea here. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Why does increasing pressure not affect the position of equilibrium in a reaction involving only solids and liquids?

options.A: Because solids and liquids are incompressible enough that pressure change has negligible directional effect

answer_text: Because solids and liquids are incompressible enough that pressure change has negligible directional effect

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why pressure usually has little effect on equilibria without gases.
explanation.principle: Pressure mainly affects equilibria through changes in gas volume.
steps: Gases are easily compressed, so pressure changes matter strongly there.
Solids and liquids change volume very little under ordinary pressure changes.
So pressure does not significantly favour one side over the other in such systems.
Therefore option A is correct.
explanation.final_answer: A. Because solids and liquids are incompressible enough that pressure change has negligible directional effect
explanation.simple_explanation: When equilibrium is disturbed, the position shifts in the direction that tends to oppose the change. That is the key exam idea here. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: A fall in temperature causes a particular equilibrium to shift to the right. Which conclusion is most reasonable?

options.D: The number of moles of gas on the right is smaller

explanation.question_restate: We are to infer the heat nature of the forward reaction from the direction of shift on cooling.
explanation.principle: Cooling favours the exothermic direction.
steps: Lowering temperature removes heat from the system.
The system responds by favouring the direction that produces heat.
If the shift is to the right, the forward direction must be exothermic.
Hence option A is correct.
explanation.final_answer: A. The forward reaction is exothermic
explanation.simple_explanation: When equilibrium is disturbed, the position shifts in the direction that tends to oppose the change. That is the key exam idea here. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: In a closed equilibrium system, the factor that always changes the value of the equilibrium constant is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the factor that alters the equilibrium constant itself.
explanation.principle: The equilibrium constant depends on temperature for a given reaction.
steps: Pressure and concentration may shift the position of equilibrium.
A catalyst changes rate only.
Temperature changes the energetic balance and therefore changes K.
So temperature is the correct answer.
explanation.final_answer: A. temperature
explanation.simple_explanation: When equilibrium is disturbed, the position shifts in the direction that tends to oppose the change. That is the key exam idea here. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Dynamic equilibrium in a chemical system is attained when

options.A: the concentrations of reactants and products become zero
options.B: the forward and backward reactions proceed at equal rates
options.C: the forward reaction stops and only the backward reaction continues

answer_text: the forward and backward reactions proceed at equal rates

explanation.question_restate: We are to define the condition for dynamic equilibrium.
explanation.principle: At dynamic equilibrium, both forward and backward reactions continue, but at equal rates.
steps: Equilibrium does not mean no reaction occurs.
Particles continue colliding and changing both ways.
The key balance is equality of forward and reverse reaction rates.
That is the defining condition of dynamic equilibrium.
explanation.final_answer: B. the forward and backward reactions proceed at equal rates
explanation.simple_explanation: Dynamic equilibrium looks unchanged to the eye, but reactions are still going on in both directions. Keep that picture in mind. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: At dynamic equilibrium, the concentration of each substance remains constant because

options.A: the substances have been used up
options.B: the rates of the forward and backward reactions are equal

answer_text: the rates of the forward and backward reactions are equal

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why concentrations stop changing at equilibrium.
explanation.principle: Equal forward and backward rates mean each substance is formed as fast as it is consumed.
steps: Reactants are still being used up in the forward direction.
But they are also being re-formed in the backward direction at the same rate.
So there is no net change in concentration.
That is why concentrations remain constant.
explanation.final_answer: B. the rates of the forward and backward reactions are equal
explanation.simple_explanation: Dynamic equilibrium looks unchanged to the eye, but reactions are still going on in both directions. Keep that picture in mind. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Which statement about dynamic equilibrium is correct?

options.A: It can exist only in open systems
options.B: It is a state of rest at the particle level
options.C: It requires both forward and backward reactions to continue in a closed system
options.D: It means reactants and products are always present in equal masses

answer_text: It requires both forward and backward reactions to continue in a closed system

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose the accurate statement about dynamic equilibrium.
explanation.principle: Dynamic equilibrium requires ongoing forward and reverse reactions with no net change, typically in a closed system.
steps: Open systems often allow substances to escape, which prevents true equilibrium.
At the particle level, reactions continue; the system is not at rest.
Equal masses are not required.
Therefore option C is correct.
explanation.final_answer: C. It requires both forward and backward reactions to continue in a closed system
explanation.simple_explanation: Dynamic equilibrium looks unchanged to the eye, but reactions are still going on in both directions. Keep that picture in mind. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: The word 'dynamic' in dynamic equilibrium emphasizes that

options.A: the reactants are moving to another container
options.B: changes are still occurring microscopically even though the macroscopic properties remain constant

answer_text: changes are still occurring microscopically even though the macroscopic properties remain constant

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the meaning of the word 'dynamic' in this context.
explanation.principle: Dynamic refers to continual microscopic change, not visible overall change.
steps: At equilibrium, molecules still react in both directions.
Because the rates are equal, the overall appearance stays constant.
So the system is active microscopically but constant macroscopically.
That is why the equilibrium is called dynamic.
explanation.final_answer: B. changes are still occurring microscopically even though the macroscopic properties remain constant
explanation.simple_explanation: Dynamic equilibrium looks unchanged to the eye, but reactions are still going on in both directions. Keep that picture in mind. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: A mixture at equilibrium is colourless. This does NOT mean that

options.A: forward and backward reactions are proceeding at equal rates
options.C: the system is static and no reaction is occurring
options.D: the system may remain unchanged in appearance

answer_text: the system is static and no reaction is occurring

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the statement that does not follow from an equilibrium system.
explanation.principle: Dynamic equilibrium is not a static state in which chemistry has stopped.
steps: A constant appearance may be observed at equilibrium.
Forward and reverse reactions still continue equally.
Therefore 'no reaction is occurring' is false.
Option C is the incorrect statement.
explanation.final_answer: C. the system is static and no reaction is occurring
explanation.simple_explanation: Dynamic equilibrium looks unchanged to the eye, but reactions are still going on in both directions. Keep that picture in mind. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Which observation is most consistent with a system at dynamic equilibrium?

options.A: The colour, pressure, and concentration remain constant with time in a closed vessel
options.B: Gas continues to escape from the flask

answer_text: The colour, pressure, and concentration remain constant with time in a closed vessel

explanation.question_restate: We are to select the observation that matches dynamic equilibrium.
explanation.principle: At equilibrium in a closed system, measurable properties remain constant over time.
steps: No net macroscopic change occurs at equilibrium.
If gas escapes, the system is not truly closed.
Complete disappearance of reactants is not expected in a reversible equilibrium.
Hence option A best fits dynamic equilibrium.
explanation.final_answer: A. The colour, pressure, and concentration remain constant with time in a closed vessel
explanation.simple_explanation: Dynamic equilibrium looks unchanged to the eye, but reactions are still going on in both directions. Keep that picture in mind. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: At dynamic equilibrium, the rate of formation of products is

options.A: greater than the rate of decomposition of products
options.B: equal to the rate at which products are converted back to reactants
options.D: always twice the rate of disappearance of reactants

answer_text: equal to the rate at which products are converted back to reactants

explanation.question_restate: We are to compare product formation and product consumption at equilibrium.
explanation.principle: Equal forward and reverse reaction rates define dynamic equilibrium.
steps: Products are formed by the forward reaction.
Products are consumed by the backward reaction.
At equilibrium, these opposing rates are equal.
So the rate of product formation equals the rate of its conversion back.
explanation.final_answer: B. equal to the rate at which products are converted back to reactants
explanation.simple_explanation: Dynamic equilibrium looks unchanged to the eye, but reactions are still going on in both directions. Keep that picture in mind. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: The best reason a closed system is required for dynamic equilibrium is that

options.B: both reactants and products must remain in contact for both directions to continue
options.C: a closed system creates a catalyst automatically

answer_text: both reactants and products must remain in contact for both directions to continue

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why closed systems are important for dynamic equilibrium.
explanation.principle: Equilibrium depends on the continued presence of all relevant species in the reaction mixture.
steps: If products leave, the reverse reaction becomes difficult or impossible.
If reactants leave, the forward reaction is disturbed too strongly.
A closed system retains the substances involved.
Therefore it allows both directions to continue until equilibrium is set up.
explanation.final_answer: B. both reactants and products must remain in contact for both directions to continue
explanation.simple_explanation: Dynamic equilibrium looks unchanged to the eye, but reactions are still going on in both directions. Keep that picture in mind. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Which statement best explains why concentrations remain unchanged at equilibrium even though reactions continue?

options.B: Each reactant is regenerated at the same rate it is consumed, and each product is consumed at the same rate it is formed

answer_text: Each reactant is regenerated at the same rate it is consumed, and each product is consumed at the same rate it is formed

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain constant concentration despite ongoing reactions.
explanation.principle: No net concentration change occurs when formation and consumption rates balance for each substance.
steps: Forward reaction consumes reactants and forms products.
Backward reaction forms reactants and consumes products.
When these rates are equal, amounts remain constant overall.
That is why concentrations do not change at equilibrium.
explanation.final_answer: B. Each reactant is regenerated at the same rate it is consumed, and each product is consumed at the same rate it is formed
explanation.simple_explanation: Dynamic equilibrium looks unchanged to the eye, but reactions are still going on in both directions. Keep that picture in mind. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: At dynamic equilibrium in a gaseous system, the pressure remains constant provided that

options.A: the vessel is open to the atmosphere
options.B: temperature and volume are kept constant while forward and backward reactions proceed at equal rates

answer_text: temperature and volume are kept constant while forward and backward reactions proceed at equal rates

explanation.question_restate: We are to state the condition under which pressure stays constant at equilibrium.
explanation.principle: If temperature and volume are fixed, equal opposing rates give constant composition and hence constant pressure.
steps: Pressure in a gas mixture depends on temperature, volume, and amount/composition of gas.
At equilibrium, composition is constant when rates are equal.
With temperature and volume also constant, pressure remains constant.
So option B is correct.
explanation.final_answer: B. temperature and volume are kept constant while forward and backward reactions proceed at equal rates
explanation.simple_explanation: Dynamic equilibrium looks unchanged to the eye, but reactions are still going on in both directions. Keep that picture in mind. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: A student writes, 'At equilibrium, the forward and reverse reactions have equal concentrations.' The main error is that equilibrium requires equality of

explanation.question_restate: We are to correct the student's statement about equilibrium.
explanation.principle: Dynamic equilibrium is defined by equal forward and backward rates, not equal concentrations.
steps: Concentrations of reactants and products may be different.
The forward and backward reactions are not substances whose concentrations are compared.
What must be equal are the rates of the two opposing reactions.
Hence option C is the correct correction.
explanation.final_answer: C. rates, not necessarily concentrations
explanation.simple_explanation: Dynamic equilibrium looks unchanged to the eye, but reactions are still going on in both directions. Keep that picture in mind. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Which one of the following must be true at dynamic equilibrium?

options.A: The amounts of reactants and products are equal
options.D: There is no net change in measurable properties

answer_text: There is no net change in measurable properties

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a statement that must hold true at equilibrium.
explanation.principle: Because opposing reaction rates are equal, the system shows no net macroscopic change.
steps: Equal amounts are not compulsory.
Neither direction has stopped.
What is observed is constancy of measurable properties such as concentration and colour.
Therefore option D must be true.
explanation.final_answer: D. There is no net change in measurable properties
explanation.simple_explanation: Dynamic equilibrium looks unchanged to the eye, but reactions are still going on in both directions. Keep that picture in mind. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: If a tracer is used to label one reactant in an equilibrium mixture, the label may later appear in the product. This supports the idea that equilibrium is

explanation.question_restate: We are to interpret what tracer movement tells us about equilibrium.
explanation.principle: Movement of labelled atoms from reactant to product shows that reaction continues while equilibrium is maintained.
steps: A tracer can reveal ongoing chemical change even when there is no visible overall change.
If labelled atoms appear in products, the forward reaction is still happening.
At equilibrium the reverse reaction also continues.
Thus the evidence supports a dynamic equilibrium.
explanation.final_answer: B. dynamic
explanation.simple_explanation: Dynamic equilibrium looks unchanged to the eye, but reactions are still going on in both directions. Keep that picture in mind. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: A reaction mixture has reached equilibrium. Which statement about particle behaviour is correct?

options.C: Particles continue colliding and reacting in both directions
options.D: All particles have the same kinetic energy

answer_text: Particles continue colliding and reacting in both directions

explanation.question_restate: We are to describe what particles do when equilibrium has been reached.
explanation.principle: Equilibrium involves continuing molecular collisions and reactions in both directions.
steps: Molecules in liquids and gases do not stop moving.
Collisions continue as long as temperature is above absolute zero.
At equilibrium, some collisions produce products and some reform reactants.
Therefore option C is correct.
explanation.final_answer: C. Particles continue colliding and reacting in both directions
explanation.simple_explanation: Dynamic equilibrium looks unchanged to the eye, but reactions are still going on in both directions. Keep that picture in mind. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Why is dynamic equilibrium not possible once a product escapes irreversibly from the system?

options.B: Because the backward reaction can no longer balance the forward reaction properly
options.C: Because catalysts cannot work in open systems

answer_text: Because the backward reaction can no longer balance the forward reaction properly

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why escape of product prevents true dynamic equilibrium.
explanation.principle: If product is lost, the reverse reaction cannot be sustained enough to balance the forward reaction.
steps: Equilibrium needs products to remain available for backward change.
When product escapes irreversibly, the reverse reaction is weakened.
So equal forward and backward rates cannot be maintained.
Therefore dynamic equilibrium is not established properly.
explanation.final_answer: B. Because the backward reaction can no longer balance the forward reaction properly
explanation.simple_explanation: Dynamic equilibrium looks unchanged to the eye, but reactions are still going on in both directions. Keep that picture in mind. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: The concentrations of reactants and products become constant at equilibrium, but the reaction is still described as dynamic because

options.A: the particles are moving between laboratories
options.B: forward and backward reactions continue simultaneously

answer_text: forward and backward reactions continue simultaneously

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain again why equilibrium is called dynamic despite constant concentrations.
explanation.principle: The constant concentrations arise from equal simultaneous opposing reactions, not from a stopped reaction.
steps: Forward reaction continues to form products.
Backward reaction continues to form reactants.
Because the two occur simultaneously at equal rates, concentrations remain constant.
That ongoing two-way activity makes the equilibrium dynamic.
explanation.final_answer: B. forward and backward reactions continue simultaneously
explanation.simple_explanation: Dynamic equilibrium looks unchanged to the eye, but reactions are still going on in both directions. Keep that picture in mind. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Le Chatelier’s principle is best stated as: when a system at equilibrium is disturbed, the system

options.B: shifts in a direction that tends to oppose the disturbance
options.C: must produce equal amounts of all substances
options.D: changes only if a catalyst is present

answer_text: shifts in a direction that tends to oppose the disturbance

explanation.question_restate: We are to state Le Chatelier’s principle correctly.
explanation.principle: An equilibrium system responds to a change by tending to reduce the effect of that change.
steps: A change in concentration, pressure, or temperature disturbs equilibrium.
The system does not ignore the change.
Instead, it shifts in the direction that partially opposes the disturbance.
That is the content of Le Chatelier’s principle.
explanation.final_answer: B. shifts in a direction that tends to oppose the disturbance
explanation.simple_explanation: Le Chatelier’s principle says an equilibrium system responds to a disturbance by shifting in the direction that reduces the effect of that disturbance. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: According to Le Chatelier’s principle, adding more NH₃ to N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) ⇌ 2NH₃(g) will cause the equilibrium to shift

options.A: to the right to form still more NH₃
options.B: to the left to consume some NH₃
options.C: nowhere because NH₃ is a gas

answer_text: to the left to consume some NH₃

explanation.question_restate: We are to apply Le Chatelier’s principle to the addition of a product.
explanation.principle: When a product is added, the system shifts to use up some of the added product.
steps: NH₃ is on the product side.
Adding it disturbs the equilibrium.
The system responds by favouring the reverse direction which consumes NH₃.
So the equilibrium shifts to the left.
explanation.final_answer: B. to the left to consume some NH₃
explanation.simple_explanation: Le Chatelier’s principle says an equilibrium system responds to a disturbance by shifting in the direction that reduces the effect of that disturbance. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: For the equilibrium 2SO₂(g) + O₂(g) ⇌ 2SO₃(g), increasing pressure shifts the equilibrium right because the system tends to

options.A: increase the number of gaseous molecules
options.B: decrease the number of gaseous molecules

answer_text: decrease the number of gaseous molecules

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the pressure effect using Le Chatelier’s principle.
explanation.principle: An increase in pressure is opposed by shifting toward the side with fewer gas molecules.
steps: Higher pressure means the system is being forced into a smaller effective volume.
The side with fewer gaseous particles reduces that stress better.
In this equilibrium, the product side has fewer gas moles.
Therefore the system shifts right.
explanation.final_answer: B. decrease the number of gaseous molecules
explanation.simple_explanation: Le Chatelier’s principle says an equilibrium system responds to a disturbance by shifting in the direction that reduces the effect of that disturbance. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: If heat is removed from an exothermic equilibrium system, Le Chatelier’s principle predicts that the system will

options.A: shift in the exothermic direction to produce more heat
options.B: shift in the endothermic direction to absorb more heat

answer_text: shift in the exothermic direction to produce more heat

explanation.question_restate: We are to predict the effect of removing heat from an exothermic equilibrium system.
explanation.principle: Cooling is opposed by shifting toward the direction that releases heat.
steps: An exothermic direction produces heat.
Removing heat lowers the temperature.
To oppose this change, the system favours the heat-producing direction.
So it shifts in the exothermic direction.
explanation.final_answer: A. shift in the exothermic direction to produce more heat
explanation.simple_explanation: Le Chatelier’s principle says an equilibrium system responds to a disturbance by shifting in the direction that reduces the effect of that disturbance. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Le Chatelier’s principle helps to predict

options.B: the direction in which an equilibrium will shift after a disturbance
options.C: the exact rate law of every reaction
options.D: the atomic number of an element

answer_text: the direction in which an equilibrium will shift after a disturbance

explanation.question_restate: We are to state what Le Chatelier’s principle is used for.
explanation.principle: The principle is a qualitative guide for predicting the direction of equilibrium shift.
steps: It does not directly give atomic numbers or exact rate equations.
Its main use is to tell how equilibrium responds to disturbance.
That means predicting the direction of shift.
Hence option B is correct.
explanation.final_answer: B. the direction in which an equilibrium will shift after a disturbance
explanation.simple_explanation: Le Chatelier’s principle says an equilibrium system responds to a disturbance by shifting in the direction that reduces the effect of that disturbance. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: A rise in concentration of a reactant disturbs equilibrium. By Le Chatelier’s principle, the system responds by favouring

options.A: the direction that consumes the added reactant
options.B: the direction that forms more of the same reactant

answer_text: the direction that consumes the added reactant

explanation.question_restate: We are to apply Le Chatelier’s principle to added reactant.
explanation.principle: The system shifts to reduce the effect of increased reactant concentration by using up some of it.
steps: Extra reactant means a concentration disturbance.
A shift that consumes that reactant opposes the change.
That corresponds to the direction in which the reactant is used.
So option A is correct.
explanation.final_answer: A. the direction that consumes the added reactant
explanation.simple_explanation: Le Chatelier’s principle says an equilibrium system responds to a disturbance by shifting in the direction that reduces the effect of that disturbance. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: In the equilibrium H₂(g) + I₂(g) ⇌ 2HI(g), increasing pressure produces little shift because

options.A: Le Chatelier’s principle does not apply to iodine systems
options.B: there is no change in total gaseous moles between the two sides
options.C: hydrogen iodide is always a solid

answer_text: there is no change in total gaseous moles between the two sides

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the small pressure effect using Le Chatelier’s principle.
explanation.principle: If both sides contain the same total gaseous moles, pressure change gives no side an advantage.
steps: Le Chatelier’s principle says the system shifts to reduce the pressure increase.
But with equal gaseous mole numbers, either side responds similarly.
So there is no meaningful positional shift due to pressure.
Hence option B is correct.
explanation.final_answer: B. there is no change in total gaseous moles between the two sides
explanation.simple_explanation: Le Chatelier’s principle says an equilibrium system responds to a disturbance by shifting in the direction that reduces the effect of that disturbance. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Which statement correctly applies Le Chatelier’s principle to catalysts?

options.A: A catalyst shifts equilibrium toward the products only
options.B: A catalyst shifts equilibrium toward the reactants only
options.C: A catalyst does not change the equilibrium position but helps the system reach equilibrium faster
options.D: A catalyst increases the equilibrium constant

answer_text: A catalyst does not change the equilibrium position but helps the system reach equilibrium faster

explanation.question_restate: We are to use Le Chatelier-type reasoning to decide the role of a catalyst at equilibrium.
explanation.principle: A catalyst lowers activation energy for both directions, so it does not favour one side over the other.
steps: Both forward and backward reactions are speeded up.
Because both are affected, there is no selective shift in position.
The system only gets to equilibrium sooner.
Therefore option C is the correct statement.
explanation.final_answer: C. A catalyst does not change the equilibrium position but helps the system reach equilibrium faster
explanation.simple_explanation: Le Chatelier’s principle says an equilibrium system responds to a disturbance by shifting in the direction that reduces the effect of that disturbance. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: If extra CO₂ is introduced into the equilibrium CaCO₃(s) ⇌ CaO(s) + CO₂(g), the system tends to

options.C: remain unaffected because solids are present

explanation.question_restate: We are to apply Le Chatelier’s principle to addition of carbon dioxide.
explanation.principle: Adding a gaseous product shifts the equilibrium to the side that consumes that product.
steps: CO₂ is on the product side.
Adding more CO₂ disturbs the equilibrium.
The system responds by shifting left, where CO₂ is used up to form CaCO₃.
Hence more CaCO₃ tends to form.
explanation.final_answer: A. form more CaCO₃
explanation.simple_explanation: Le Chatelier’s principle says an equilibrium system responds to a disturbance by shifting in the direction that reduces the effect of that disturbance. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: By Le Chatelier’s principle, decreasing the concentration of a reactant in an equilibrium mixture causes the system to shift

options.A: towards the side that replaces some of the removed reactant
options.B: towards the side that removes more reactant
options.D: only if the reaction is irreversible

answer_text: towards the side that replaces some of the removed reactant

explanation.question_restate: We are to predict the effect of removing some reactant from an equilibrium system.
explanation.principle: The system opposes the decrease by shifting in the direction that forms more of the removed reactant.
steps: Removal of reactant is a disturbance.
To oppose it, the system tries to restore some of that reactant.
That means the reverse direction is favoured if the reactant lies on the left.
So option A expresses the idea correctly.
explanation.final_answer: A. towards the side that replaces some of the removed reactant
explanation.simple_explanation: Le Chatelier’s principle says an equilibrium system responds to a disturbance by shifting in the direction that reduces the effect of that disturbance. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: The reason high pressure favours ammonia formation in N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) ⇌ 2NH₃(g) is that the system tends to

options.B: reduce the effect of the pressure increase by moving to the side with fewer gas molecules

answer_text: reduce the effect of the pressure increase by moving to the side with fewer gas molecules

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain ammonia formation under pressure in terms of Le Chatelier’s principle.
explanation.principle: The system responds to increased pressure by favouring the side with fewer gaseous molecules.
steps: The reactant side has 4 moles of gas in total.
The product side has 2 moles of gas.
To reduce pressure stress, the equilibrium shifts to the side with fewer molecules.
Thus ammonia formation is favoured.
explanation.final_answer: B. reduce the effect of the pressure increase by moving to the side with fewer gas molecules
explanation.simple_explanation: Le Chatelier’s principle says an equilibrium system responds to a disturbance by shifting in the direction that reduces the effect of that disturbance. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Suppose the forward reaction in an equilibrium is endothermic. According to Le Chatelier’s principle, heating the system should

options.D: decrease the equilibrium constant to zero

explanation.question_restate: We are to apply Le Chatelier’s principle to heating an endothermic forward reaction.
explanation.principle: Adding heat favours the heat-absorbing direction.
steps: An endothermic direction uses up heat.
Heating the system adds heat to the equilibrium mixture.
The shift that opposes the change is the one that absorbs this added heat.
So the forward direction is favoured.
explanation.final_answer: A. favour the forward reaction
explanation.simple_explanation: Le Chatelier’s principle says an equilibrium system responds to a disturbance by shifting in the direction that reduces the effect of that disturbance. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: Which disturbance is best opposed by shifting towards the side with fewer gas molecules?

options.D: Removal of solid from a heterogeneous equilibrium

explanation.question_restate: We are to match a disturbance with the type of shift expected by Le Chatelier’s principle.
explanation.principle: A pressure increase is opposed by favouring the side with fewer gas molecules.
steps: Pressure concerns gas volume and gas particle crowding.
The best way to reduce that stress is to favour the side with fewer gas molecules.
Temperature and catalyst do not fit that specific rule.
Therefore option A is correct.
explanation.final_answer: A. Increase in pressure
explanation.simple_explanation: Le Chatelier’s principle says an equilibrium system responds to a disturbance by shifting in the direction that reduces the effect of that disturbance. For more help, check our "Tutorial section".

question: A system at equilibrium is compressed, yet no shift occurs. A likely reason is that

options.B: the number of gaseous moles is the same on both sides

answer_text: the number of gaseous moles is the same on both sides

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why compression may fail to shift a gaseous equilibrium.
explanation.principle: If gaseous mole numbers are equal on both sides, compression does not favour one side over the other.
steps: Le Chatelier’s principle still applies.
The system would shift only if one side reduced the pressure effect better.
With equal gaseous moles, neither side has that advantage.
So option B is the sensible explanation.
